summary:
The Wars of the Roses (1455–1485 AD) were a series of civil wars for the English throne between the rival royal houses of Lancaster (whose badge was a red rose) and York (white rose), fought during the reigns of the weak Lancastrian King Henry VI and the subsequent Yorkist and then Tudor monarchs, representing the final convulsion of the medieval English nobility before the Tudor dynasty established more centralized royal authority. The wars were primarily a conflict within the English aristocracy — fought by armies of noble retinues rather than conscript forces — and had more limited civilian impact than comparable continental conflicts.

The conflict's origins lay in the disputed succession following the Lancastrian line's weakness: Henry VI suffered from mental illness (catatonic episodes) and was a manifestly poor ruler, creating openings for the Yorkist claimant Richard Duke of York. The wars produced dramatic reversals of fortune — the deposition of Henry VI twice, the brief reign of Edward IV, the mysterious disappearance of the 'Princes in the Tower' under Richard III — that Shakespeare dramatized in his history plays, creating the most influential historical mythology in the English language. The wars ended at Bosworth Field (1485) when the Lancastrian claimant Henry Tudor defeated and killed Richard III, founding the Tudor Dynasty as Henry VII. Henry's marriage to Elizabeth of York united the rival roses and ended the conflict. The wars decimated many old noble families through battle casualties and subsequent attainders, inadvertently strengthening royal authority by reducing the power of the great magnates who had made the monarchy vulnerable.
